title: Repairing a garden fence panel
subtitle: Replace damaged boards and tighten a failing fence frame
excerpt: How to repair a close-board or lap fence panel: replacing individual broken boards, repairing a split rail, re-fixing a panel that has come away from the post, and treating the timber to extend the life of the repair.

glossaryTerms.definition: A triangular-section timber rail fitted horizontally between fence posts. The featheredge boards of a close-board fence nail to the front face of the arris rail. Common points of failure are where the rail fits into the mortise in the post and where the grain is short near a knot.

content.text: Fence panels fail in two main ways: individual boards split or rot, and the structural rails or posts fail at the joints. A panel that has blown off its posts after a storm is usually structurally sound and needs only re-fixing. A panel with spreading rot in the boards is better replaced in its entirety.
Replacing individual boards on a close-board fence
On a close-board fence, featheredge boards are nailed to the

with the thick edge overlapping the thin edge of the next board. To replace a single board, lever off the damaged board with a wrecking bar, taking care not to split the boards on either side. Cut the replacement board to length and nail in place with 50 mm galvanised round nails at each rail position, maintaining the same overlap as the adjacent boards (typically 12 to 19 mm). Treat all cut ends with a timber preservative before fixing.

A split arris rail can be repaired with a proprietary arris rail bracket: a galvanised metal bracket that bolts around the rail and screws to the face of the post. Clean the break, apply PVA to both faces of the split, bring back together, and fit the bracket over the joint. The bracket takes the load; the PVA prevents moisture entering the crack. Allow the PVA to cure before re-fixing the fence boards.
Re-fixing a panel to a post

A lapped fence panel that has come away from its post clips into slot-head brackets screwed to the post face, or rests in grooves in timber posts. If the brackets have pulled out, fit new 40 mm panel brackets with 50 mm screws into sound timber, or pre-drill and drive coach screws through the panel frame into the post. Check the panel is vertical before final tightening.

All new and repaired timber should receive a coat of pressure-treatment preservative stain. Apply with a brush to all faces, including the back face and both ends: the end grain is the primary route for moisture entry. A treated fence panel needs re-treating every 2 to 3 years. A panel above ground level on well-drained soil will last 15 to 20 years with maintenance; one sitting in standing water at the base will fail in 5 years.

attrs.cause: Nail driven too close to the end of the board, or the board too dry and close-grained.
attrs.fix: Pre-drill a pilot hole slightly smaller than the nail shank at each end-grain fixing point.

attrs.cause: Post is itself leaning or rotted at ground level.
attrs.fix: A leaning post must be re-set or replaced before the panel repair is worthwhile. Pack the post base with concrete if the post is sound; replace the post if the base is rotted.
